sanskrit_devanagari:
पुण्यो गन्धः पृथिव्यां च तेजश्चास्मि विभावसौ ।
जीवनं सर्वभूतेषु तपश्चास्मि तपस्विषु ॥ ९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
puṇyo gandhaḥ pṛthivyāṁ ca tejaś cāsmi vibhāvasau |
jīvanaṁ sarva-bhūteṣu tapaś cāsmi tapasviṣu || 9 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.009 = MBh_06,029.009). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: I am the original fragrance of the earth, and I am the heat in fire. I am the life of all that lives, and I am the penances of all ascetics.
I am the pure fragrance of the Earth, and the brilliance in fire. I am the life-force in all beings, and the penance of the ascetics.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Puṇya means that which is not decomposed; puṇya is original. Everything in the material world has a certain flavor or fragrance, as the flavor and fragrance in a flower, or in the earth, in water, in fire, in the air, etc.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.9 I am also the puṇyaḥ, sweet; gandhaḥ, fragrance; pṛthivyām, in the earth. The earth is dependent on Me who am its fragrance. The natural sweetness of smell in the earth is cited by way of suggesting sweetness of taste of water etc. as well.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara extends the essence-rule from 7.8: the earth is dependent on Krishna who is its fragrance. The 'sweet' or 'natural' qualifier (puṇyaḥ) signals that Krishna is the unmodified, original property — not the contamination or distortion that arises from contact with particulars.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.8 – 7.11 All these entities with their peculiar characteristic are born from Me alone. They depend on Me; inasmuch as they constitute My body, they exist in Me alone. Thus I alone exist while all of them are only My modes.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja's commentary covers 7.8-7.11 as a unit (see 7.8). For 7.9 specifically: fragrance-in-earth, brilliance-in-fire, life-in-beings, austerity-in-ascetics — each is a peculiar characteristic of the thing, and each is Krishna's mode.

positions.rendering: the original fragrance of the earth
the pure fragrance of the Earth
the sweet (natural, unmodified) fragrance — Krishna is the original property without the distortion from particular contact
(treated as part of 7.8-7.11 unit) — fragrance-in-earth is Krishna's mode

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Puṇyaḥ admits multiple defensible renderings: 'pure', 'sweet', 'original', 'pleasant'. Prabhupada's 'original' picks up Shankara's emphasis on the unmodified property; Mukundananda's 'pure' picks up the contrast with corrupted particulars. The structural claim is the same across translations: Krishna is the property in its pristine form, not the property as distorted by particular contacts.

literal_meaning: I am the pure fragrance in earth, and I am the brilliance in fire; I am the life in all beings, and I am the austerity in ascetics.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The middle of the I-am-the-X-in-Y sequence (7.8-7.11). Krishna continues to name durable properties: fragrance-in-earth, brilliance-in-fire, life-in-beings, austerity-in-ascetics. The pattern is consistent: the property named is the one without which the thing is not what it is. Shankara reads each as essence (sāra); Ramanuja as mode (prakāra). The verse moves from physical elements (water, sun, moon, ether — 7.8) into more pervasive properties (life-in-beings, austerity-in-ascetics) — the chapter is widening the reach.
